words.example: Estoy aquí — I'm here
Está allí — It's there
Gira a la izquierda — Turn left
A la derecha — To the right
Está arriba — It's up there
Ven abajo — Come down
Sigue todo derecho — Keep going straight
words.note: The default form across Latin America

words.example: Siga derecho dos cuadras — Go straight for two blocks
words.note: Common short form in Mexico and Central America. 'Cuadra' = block

words.example: Sigue todo recto — Keep going straight
words.note: The Spanish form — you'll mostly hear this in Spain

words.example: Dame uno — Give me one
Solo quiero agua — I just want water
Solo una vez — Just once
¿Puedes repetir otra vez? — Can you repeat again?
words.note: One of the most useful phrases day-to-day

words.example: A veces como fuera — Sometimes I eat out
Esta vez yo pago — This time I'm paying
La próxima vez te aviso — Next time I'll let you know
words.note: Próximo/a = next/upcoming. Also: el próximo lunes = next Monday

words.example: El lunes voy al mercado — Monday I'm going to the market
Nos vemos el martes — See you Tuesday
Miércoles es el día más largo — Wednesday is the longest day
El jueves tengo una cita — Thursday I have an appointment
¡Por fin es viernes! — Finally it's Friday!
El sábado descansamos — Saturday we rest
El domingo es día de familia — Sunday is family day
Hoy es lunes — Today is Monday
Mañana es martes — Tomorrow is Tuesday
Ayer fue domingo — Yesterday was Sunday
Esta semana estoy muy ocupado — This week I'm very busy
La semana pasada fue genial — Last week was great
La semana que viene te llamo — I'll call you next week
La próxima semana viajo — Next week I travel
words.note: Equally common in Latin America

words.example: ¿Qué haces el fin de semana? — What are you doing this weekend?
Los fines de semana descanso — On weekends I rest
Entre semana trabajo mucho — During the week I work a lot
Llegué hace una semana — I arrived a week ago
Dentro de una semana nos vamos — In a week's time we leave
¿Qué día es hoy? Hoy es viernes — What day is today? Today is Friday
Cero grados — Zero degrees
Solo uno — Just one
Dos cafés, por favor — Two coffees, please
Tres días — Three days
Cuatro personas — Four people
Cinco minutos — Five minutes
Seis pesos — Six pesos
Siete días a la semana — Seven days a week
Ocho horas de sueño — Eight hours of sleep
Nueve de la mañana — Nine in the morning
Diez minutos más — Ten more minutes
Once personas — Eleven people
Doce del mediodía — Twelve noon
Trece años — Thirteen years
Catorce días — Fourteen days
Quince minutos — Fifteen minutes
Dieciséis pesos — Sixteen pesos
words.note: diez + y + seis fused into one word

words.example: Diecisiete años — Seventeen years
words.note: diez + y + siete fused

words.example: Dieciocho grados — Eighteen degrees
words.note: diez + y + ocho fused

words.example: Diecinueve personas — Nineteen people
words.note: diez + y + nueve fused

words.example: Veinte pesos — Twenty pesos
words.note: 21–29 fuse into one word: veintiuno, veintidós, veintitrés...

words.example: Veintiuno de marzo — March 21st
words.note: veinte + uno → veintiuno (one word)

words.example: Treinta minutos — Thirty minutes
words.note: From 31 up: tens + y + unit, always two words. treinta y uno, treinta y dos...

words.example: Treinta y dos grados — Thirty-two degrees
words.note: Pattern: [tens] + y + [unit]. Works for all numbers 31–99.

words.example: Cuarenta años — Forty years
words.note: cuarenta y tres, cuarenta y cuatro...

words.example: Cincuenta metros — Fifty metres
words.note: cincuenta y cinco, cincuenta y seis...

words.example: Sesenta segundos — Sixty seconds
words.note: sesenta y siete, sesenta y ocho...

words.example: Setenta kilómetros — Seventy kilometres
words.note: setenta y uno, setenta y dos...

words.example: Ochenta personas — Eighty people
words.note: ochenta y nueve, ochenta y ocho...

words.example: Noventa días — Ninety days
words.note: noventa y cinco, noventa y seis...

words.example: Cien pesos — One hundred pesos
words.note: 'Cien' stands alone. 'Ciento' before more: ciento veinte = 120

words.example: Doscientos metros — Two hundred metres
Mil gracias — A thousand thanks
words.note: mil doscientos = 1,200 — just stack the numbers

words.example: Un millón de razones — A million reasons
El primero de enero — The first of January
words.note: Shortens before a noun: el primer día

words.example: El segundo piso — The second floor
La tercera vez — The third time
words.note: Shortens before a noun: el tercer piso

words.example: El cuarto día — The fourth day
words.note: Same word as 'a quarter' — useful overlap!

words.example: La quinta planta — The fifth floor
El sexto sentido — The sixth sense
El séptimo día — The seventh day
El octavo lugar — Eighth place
El noveno mes — The ninth month
El décimo aniversario — The tenth anniversary
El último tren — The last train
words.note: More common in everyday speech than sexto, séptimo, etc.

words.example: Media hora — Half an hour
La mitad del tiempo — Half the time
Un tercio del precio — A third of the price
Un cuarto de hora — A quarter of an hour
words.note: Cuarto = fourth AND a quarter — same word

words.example: Dos tercios del grupo — Two thirds of the group
Tres cuartos de hora — Three quarters of an hour
words.note: Very common for telling time

words.example: El vaso está lleno — The glass is full
Perdona, ¿tienes un momento? — Excuse me, do you have a moment?
Perdone, ¿dónde está el baño? — Excuse me, where's the bathroom?
Perdón, no te oí — Sorry, I didn't hear you
words.note: The most universal everyday form across Latin America

words.example: Oye, ¿me ayudas? — Hey, can you help me?
Un café, por favor — A coffee, please
Con permiso, voy a pasar — Excuse me, I'm coming through
words.note: Very common in Mexico and Central America when passing in front of someone

words.example: Paso, paso — Coming through
Lo siento mucho — I'm very sorry
Disculpa la demora — Sorry for the delay
words.note: Slightly more LatAm-leaning than 'perdona'

words.example: Disculpe, una pregunta — Excuse me, a question
words.note: The default polite form to address a stranger in Mexico/Central America

words.example: ¿Cómo? No te oí — What? I didn't hear you
¿Perdón? ¿Puede repetir? — Pardon? Can you repeat?
No entiendo, ¿puedes hablar despacio? — I don't understand, can you speak slowly?
No entendí lo último — I didn't understand the last part
words.note: Standard preterite form across Latin America

words.example: No he entendido bien — I haven't quite understood
words.note: Present perfect — much more common in Spain than Latin America

words.example: ¿Puede repetir, por favor? — Can you repeat, please?
Más despacio, por favor — Slower, please
¿Habla inglés? — Do you speak English?
¿Qué significa esa palabra? — What does that word mean?
Habla más suave, por favor — Please speak more softly
Todo estaba delicioso, gracias — Everything was delicious, thank you
La comida estuvo riquísima — The meal was super tasty
Excelente, muchas gracias — Excellent, thank you very much
Mis cumplidos al chef — My compliments to the chef
¡Muy rico todo! — Everything was very tasty!
words.note: 'Rico' is the everyday word for tasty across Latin America

words.example: Quedó padrísimo el mole — The mole turned out awesome
words.note: Very Mexican — 'padre/padrísimo' = great/awesome

words.example: Me encantó la comida — I loved the food
El postre estaba de diez — The dessert was top-notch
words.note: Very Spanish expression. Less common in Latin America.

words.example: Me gustaría un café — I'd like a coffee

words.en: Can you give me / I'll have
words.example: ¿Me da un café, por favor? — Can I have a coffee, please?
words.note: The default way to order across Mexico and Central America

words.example: Me pone un café, por favor — Can I have a coffee, please
words.note: Very Spanish — you'll mostly hear this in Spain

words.example: Para mí, una cerveza — A beer for me
La cuenta, por favor — The check, please
Tengo mucha hambre — I'm very hungry
Tengo sed, ¿hay agua? — I'm thirsty, is there water?
¿Me trae el menú? — Can you bring the menu?
words.note: Most common term across Latin America

words.example: La carta, por favor — The menu, please
words.note: Used in Spain and parts of LatAm; refers especially to the printed menu

words.example: Una mesa para dos, por favor — A table for two, please
Vamos a comer — Let's go eat
¿Qué quieres tomar? — What do you want to drink?
words.note: 'Tomar' is the default for 'to drink' across Latin America. 'Beber' is more formal/Spain.

words.example: Beber agua es importante — Drinking water is important
words.note: More formal in LatAm; everyday in Spain

words.example: Está muy rico — It's really tasty
Muy bueno — Very good
Sabe malo — It tastes bad
Demasiado picante — Too spicy
Es suficiente, gracias — That's enough, thanks
¿Es muy picante? — Is it very spicy?
words.note: Critical in Mexico — ranges from mild to brutal

words.example: Demasiado dulce — Too sweet
¿Tiene opciones sin gluten? — Do you have gluten-free options?
Soy intolerante a la lactosa — I'm lactose intolerant
Soy alérgico a los mariscos — I'm allergic to shellfish
¿Qué hora es? — What time is it?
Es la una en punto — It's one o'clock sharp
words.note: Singular 'es' only for one o'clock

words.example: Son las tres y media — It's three thirty
words.note: Plural 'son' for all other hours

words.example: A las ocho — At eight o'clock
Las ocho y media — Half past eight
Por la mañana — In the morning
Por la tarde — In the afternoon
Por la noche — At night
Al mediodía comemos — At noon we eat
A la medianoche — At midnight
Ahora no puedo — I can't right now
Ahora mismo voy — I'm coming right now
Ahorita vengo — I'll be right back
words.note: Very common in Mexico/Central America. Note: can mean anywhere from 'right now' to 'eventually' — context matters!

words.example: Hasta luego — See you later
Antes de comer — Before eating
Después de las tres — After three
Llego pronto — I'll arrive soon
Llegué tarde — I arrived late
Muy temprano — Very early
Un momento, por favor — One moment, please
Espera un rato — Wait a while
Voy enseguida — I'm coming right away
Ya llegué — I'm here now
words.note: Tiny word, huge range of meaning

words.example: ¿Cuánto cuesta esto? — How much does this cost?
¿Cuánto es todo? — How much is it all?
words.note: Common when paying at the counter

words.example: Es muy caro — It's very expensive
Está barato — It's cheap
Un poco más, por favor — A little more, please
Un poco menos — A little less
Me lo llevo — I'll take it
¿Tiene cambio de cien? — Do you have change for a hundred?
¿Puedo pagar con tarjeta? — Can I pay by card?
Pago en efectivo — I'll pay in cash
¿Me da el recibo? — Can I have the receipt?
¿Me da la nota? — Can I have the receipt?
words.note: Common informal terms in Mexico/Central America

words.example: Tomo el autobús — I take the bus
words.note: Universal but several regional alternatives below

words.example: El camión a Oaxaca — The bus to Oaxaca
words.note: In Mexico 'el camión' often = bus (not truck!)

words.example: La guagua sale a las ocho — The bus leaves at eight
words.note: Used in Panama, Cuba, Dominican Republic, Puerto Rico

words.example: El tren llega tarde — The train is late
Voy en metro — I'm going by metro
Voy a pie — I'm going on foot
En carro tarda media hora — By car it takes half an hour
words.note: 'Carro' is standard across Mexico, Central America, Colombia, Caribbean

words.example: Vamos en coche — Let's go by car
words.note: 'Coche' is the Spanish term — sounds odd in much of LatAm

words.example: En auto es más rápido — By car is faster
words.note: 'Auto' is preferred in Argentina, Uruguay, Chile

words.example: ¿Dónde está el baño? — Where's the bathroom?
¿Cómo llego al centro? — How do I get to downtown?
Está cerca — It's near
Está muy lejos — It's very far
Gira a la derecha — Turn right
Dos cuadras más — Two more blocks
words.note: 'Cuadra' is the Latin American word for a city block. In Spain: 'manzana'

words.example: Sí, claro — Yes, of course
Claro que sí — Of course
words.note: Universal across Spanish-speaking world

words.example: Vale, hasta luego — Ok, see you later
words.note: The go-to 'ok' in Spain — uncommon in Latin America

words.example: Sale, ahí estaré — Ok, I'll be there
words.note: The default 'ok' in Mexico

words.example: Dale, nos vemos — Ok, see you
words.note: Argentina/Uruguay; also heard in Panama and parts of Central America

words.example: Por supuesto que voy — Of course I'm going
Exacto, eso es — Exactly, that's it
No, gracias — No, thanks
Para nada, no me molesta — Not at all, it doesn't bother me
En absoluto — Absolutely not
Yo tampoco — Me neither
No sé, depende — I don't know, it depends
Quizás mañana — Maybe tomorrow
Tal vez sí — Maybe yes
words.note: Slightly more common in Latin America than 'quizás'

words.example: Depende del día — It depends on the day
Tengo mucho frío — I'm very cold
words.note: Spanish uses 'have' for temperature feelings, not 'be'

words.example: Tengo calor — I'm hot
Estoy muy cansado — I'm very tired
Me duele la cabeza — My head hurts
Estoy bien, gracias — I'm fine, thanks
Estoy mal del estómago — My stomach is upset
Estoy muy contento — I'm very happy
Estoy preocupada — I'm worried
Tengo miedo de los perros — I'm afraid of dogs
Está enojado conmigo — He's angry with me
words.note: The standard form across Latin America

words.example: Está enfadado — He's upset
words.note: Spanish form — 'enojado' is what you'll hear in Mexico/Central America

words.example: Hola, ¿cómo estás? — Hi, how are you?
Buenos días, señora — Good morning, ma'am
Buenas tardes — Good afternoon
Buenas noches, hasta mañana — Good night, see you tomorrow
¿Cómo estás hoy? — How are you today?
words.note: The default greeting question in Latin America

words.example: ¿Qué tal? — How's it going?
words.note: Common in Spain; understood everywhere but less used in Mexico

words.example: ¿Qué onda, amigo? — What's up, friend?
words.note: Very Mexican / Central American casual greeting

words.example: Adiós, cuídate — Goodbye, take care
Hasta luego — See you later
Hasta mañana — See you tomorrow
Nos vemos pronto — See you soon
Cuídate mucho — Take good care
Una casa grande — A big house
Un perro pequeño — A small dog
Un café chico — A small coffee
words.note: More common than 'pequeño' in everyday LatAm speech

words.example: Mucho gusto — Pleasure (lit. much pleasure)
Un poco de azúcar — A little sugar
Bastante bien — Quite well
Un poco más — A bit more
Menos sal — Less salt
Es igual — It's the same
Mejor mañana — Better tomorrow
Peor que ayer — Worse than yesterday
Mi pareja y yo — My partner and I
Mi hija tiene cinco años — My daughter is five
Mi madre vive aquí — My mother lives here
Mi mamá cocina rico — My mom cooks great
words.note: Far more common in everyday Latin American speech than 'madre'

words.example: Mi padre trabaja mucho — My father works a lot
Mi papá es de Panamá — My dad is from Panama
words.note: Far more common in everyday Latin American speech than 'padre'

words.example: Él es mi amigo — He's my friend
Ella es de México — She's from Mexico
Ellos llegan tarde — They're arriving late
Ellas son hermanas — They are sisters
¿Ustedes hablan inglés? — Do you all speak English?
words.note: 'Ustedes' is universal 'you all' in Latin America (formal & informal)

words.example: ¿Vosotros venís? — Are you all coming?
words.note: Spain only — Latin America uses 'ustedes' for all 'you all'

words.example: Mi amiga es de Panamá — My friend is from Panama
El señor González — Mr. González
La señora López — Mrs. López
Quiero un café — I want a coffee
Necesito ayuda — I need help
Tengo dos hermanos — I have two siblings
Busco un hotel — I'm looking for a hotel
¿Puedo pasar? — Can I come in?
Sé que es tarde — I know it's late
Conozco esta ciudad — I know this city
words.note: Two different verbs for 'to know' — saber (facts) vs. conocer (people/places)

words.example: Me gusta el café — I like coffee
Prefiero el té — I prefer tea
Me encanta este lugar — I love this place
Todo bien — Everything's fine
Perfecto, nos vemos allí — Perfect, see you there
De acuerdo, lo hago yo — Agreed, I'll do it
Claro que sí — Of course
words.note: Very common in Spain and Latin America

words.example: Ok, entendido — Ok, got it
Vale, hasta luego — Ok, see you later
words.note: The go-to 'ok' in Spain, less common in Latin America

words.example: Venga, vamos — Alright, let's go
words.note: Very Spanish — used constantly for agreement, encouragement, goodbye

words.example: Entendido, lo hago ahora — Got it, I'll do it now
Comprendido, sin problema — Understood, no problem
Ya entendí, gracias — I got it now, thanks
words.note: 'Ya' adds a sense of 'just now / finally'

words.example: Quedó claro, no hay duda — It's clear, no doubt
Lo tengo, no te preocupes — Got it, don't worry
Listo, para eso estamos — Got it, that's what we're here for
words.note: Widely used across Latin America

words.example: Me parece bien, quedamos así — Sounds good, let's leave it like that
Suena bien, nos vemos el lunes — Sounds good, see you Monday
Hecho, allí estaré — Done, I'll be there
Quedamos así entonces — Let's leave it like that then
words.note: Very useful for confirming plans

words.example: Va, cuenten conmigo — Ok, count me in
Dale, nos vemos — Ok, see you
words.note: Common in Argentina/Uruguay, also heard in Panama and Central America

words.example: Sale, ahí estaré — Ok, I'll be there
words.note: The go-to 'ok' in Mexico

words.example: Anotado, lo hago hoy — Noted, I'll do it today
Tomo nota, gracias — Noted, thanks
Apuntado, no se me olvida — Noted, I won't forget
Lo tengo presente, no te preocupes — I'll keep it in mind, don't worry
Me encargo yo — I'll take care of it
Me ocupo ahora mismo — I'll handle it right now
En eso estoy — I'm on it
Ahora mismo lo hago — I'll do it right away
Está bien, como quieras — Okay, as you wish
Como prefieras, yo me adapto — As you prefer, I'll adapt
Como veas tú — As you see fit
Me sirve, sin problema — Works for me, no problem
Puede ser, no sé — Could be, I don't know
words.note: Softer than sí — implies mild agreement or uncertainty

words.example: Por mí, bien, tú decides — Fine by me, you decide
Órale, vamos — Ok, let's go
words.note: Very colloquial Mexican expression — enthusiasm or agreement

words.example: Yo jalo — I'm in
words.note: Informal Mexican slang for joining in or agreeing

words.example: Ya quedó, no te preocupes — It's sorted, don't worry
words.note: Very common in Mexico for confirming something is done

words.en: right away / sure / let's do it
words.example: De una, vamos — Sure, let's go
words.note: Common in Colombia and Argentina

words.example: Dale, nos vemos — Ok, see you
words.note: Argentina/Uruguay. Also heard in Panama and parts of Central America

words.example: Listo, para eso estamos — Done, that's what we're here for
words.note: Broadly understood across all of Latin America

words.example: Sale, ahí estaré — Ok, I'll be there
words.note: The default 'ok' in Mexico

words.example: Vale, hasta luego — Ok, see you
words.note: Spain. You'll still hear it occasionally in Latin America from Spaniards
